coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as temperature nanosensors in the physiological range
coredata.dc:creator: Hernández-Rodríguez, M.A.
Afonso, María M.
Palenzuela, J. Antonio
Martín, I.R.
Soler-Carracedo, K.
coredata.dc:description: This work shows the utility of the carbon dots, prepared from candle shoot, as promising candidates for nanothermometry devices based on temperature dependence of their emission lifetimes and of the changes of the full width at half maximum of the emission spectrum. The results indicate that both parameters show appreciable changes over the physiological temperature range (20–60°C).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Temperature nanosensors

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was partially supported by Indo-Spanish Joint Programme of Cooperation in Science and Technology ( DST-INT-Spain-P-38-11/PRI-PIBIN-2011-1153 ), MICINN ( MAT2016-46649-C4-4-P ), the Consolider-Ingenio 2010 Program MALTA ( CSD2007-0045 ), and by the EU-FEDER . M.A.Hernández-Rodríguez thanks to MICINN by FPI grant. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Deconvolution of the C1s band of the XPS spectra. b) TEM image of the Cds used in this study.

Carbon dots emission spectrum and average lifetimes obtained at room temperature as function of the detection wavelength upon 405nm excitation. The inset shows a photo of the carbon dots upon 405nm excitation.

Temperature dependence of the intensity of the emission spectrum of the carbon dots upon 405nm excitation and dependence of the increment of the FWHM of the emission band respect to the value obtained at 20°C (139nm).

Carbon dots average lifetime dependence with the temperature obtained at two different emission wavelengths upon 405nm excitation. Red and blue colors denote increase and decrease of the temperature respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as temperature nanosensors in the physiological range

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MALTA Consolider Team and Instituto de Materiales y Nanotecnología (IMN) Universidad de La Laguna

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work shows the utility of the carbon dots, prepared from candle shoot, as promising candidates for nanothermometry devices based on temperature dependence of their emission lifetimes and of the changes of the full width at half maximum of the emission spectrum. The results indicate that both parameters show appreciable changes over the physiological temperature range (20–60°C).

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon Dots (CDs) have raised interest recently due to their numerous advantages, among them it is worth mentioning their easy and cost-effective production, as well as the variety of methods to produce them such as laser irradiation, electrochemical oxidation, microwave treatment, hydrothermal treatment or strong acid oxidation [1–6]. CDs showed also important benefits for biological and optoelectronics applications. Moreover, they are heavy-metal free particles that present low toxicity, high optical absorption, chemical stability, nonblinking, high quantum yields, low photo- bleaching and biocompatibility [7–13]. All this makes CDs a promising material for fluorescence based nanosensors as already its dependence with pH or temperature (just below room temperature) has been studied [14,15]. However, optical properties of CDs remains as a matter of debate because they are highly related to the different kinds of carbon particles [8,9,16–28] like Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) [16], Carbon nanodots (CNDs) [17] and polymer dots (PDs) [23]; each one of them with different structure and photoluminescence properties (PL).

This work is mainly focused on showing the usefulness of the carbon dots as temperature nanosensors. The temperature dependence of their emission lifetimes and changes of the width at half maximum of the emission spectrum between 20° and 60° was analyzed.

CDs were prepared following literature procedures [18]. In brief, candle shoot was collected in a glass surface, scraped and refluxed in 5M HNO3 for 12h. After centrifugation (5000rpm, 20min) the light brown supernatant was treated with excess NaBH4 in order to reduce the groups on the surface of the nanoparticles, which resulted in enhanced fluorescence [29,30]. The solution was filtered through a 0.45µm filter, dialyzed against water (MWCO 3500) and used for the measurements.

The PL spectra in the UV–Vis region and decay curve lifetimes were measured exciting with a 405nm picosecond pulsed diode laser (Edinburgh Instruments EPL- 405) with a pulse width of 80ps and recorded via a fluorescence spectrometer with a single photon counting multichannel plate photomultiplier and a dedicated acquisition software (Edinburgh Instruments LifeSpec II and F900 software). The high-resolution XPS spectra were collected on an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer equipped with dual aluminum-magnesium anodes, using a monochromatized Al Kα X-ray radiation (hv = 1486.6eV) with a spot size of 650µm. TEM measurements were performed in a JEOL JEM 2100 equipment operating at 200kV.

The results from the XPS indicated a composition in the surface mainly of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and small amounts of other elements such as sodium and boron. Analysis of the C1s ( Fig. 1a) band indicates a high degree of oxidation on the surface of the Cds. TEM analysis (Fig. 1b) showed quasi-spherical particles with an average diameter of 31nm and low size dispersion (from 25 to 38nm).

Fig. 2 shows the typical broad emission spectra of the carbon dots [7], with a maximum intensity centered around 530nm [14]. The analysis of the PL of the carbon dots is a matter of discussion, because it depends on the particle sizes [31] and their surface chemistry, where the “defect sites” on the surfaces could be responsible for trapping of excited-state energy [7,11,12].

The decay curves of the emission have not exponential character as has been analyzed in other previous works [30,31]. Therefore, in order to compare the decays obtained under different conditions (detection wavelengths and temperature) it is defined the average lifetime as,
(1)
τ
AVG
=
∫
0
∞
tI
(
t
)

∫
0
∞
I
(
t
)
dt
where I(t) is the emission obtained as function of the time.

In the Fig. 2 are also included the average lifetimes obtained as function of the detection wavelength under 405nm excitation. It can be seen that the highest values of the average lifetime coincides with the maximum of the emission spectrum. Also, a photo of the carbon dots upon 405nm wavelength excitation is added, where can be easily appreciated its white emission fluorescence.

The FWHM of the emission band shown in Fig. 2 has a value of 139nm at 20°C and it increases with the temperature up to approximately a value of 145nm at 60°C. The temperature dependence of the increment of the FWHM (Full Width at half maximum) of the emission band can be seen in Fig. 3. On the other hand, in this temperature range the emission band intensity decreases with the temperature (see Fig. 3). These results are in good agreement with the literature, where Wang et al. [6] and Yum et al. [14] found similar decrease behavior of the intensity with the increase of the temperature in carbon dots in the range between 20 and 60°C and between cryogenic to room temperature respectively.

The results concerning the broadening of the emission bandwidth show an increment of 5,6nm for the range between 20 and 60°C (approximately 0,16nm/°C). A similar increment was obtained by Pyng Yu et al. [14] at low temperatures in a range 4 times larger (77–300K).

Fig. 4 shows the dependence of the average lifetime obtained at two different emission wavelengths upon 405nm excitation. It can be seen that the average lifetime obtained at 515nm changes with the temperature more than that obtained at 433nm. The PL relaxation dynamics becomes quicker as long as the temperature increases, which could be related to non-radiative decay process. This non-radiative process could be due to the thermal activation of non-radiative trapping [14].

These noticeable changes of the average lifetime with the temperature of the carbon dots are also suitable as a temperature calibration, i.e, temperature nanosensors. It is noteworthy that measuring the average lifetimes or FWHM with temperature are more useful that measuring the emission intensity as a function of the temperature, because these parameters are independent of how these carbon dots are excited i.e, it does not import how many particles of the sample are excited (partial excitation of the sample), which can affect the relative emission intensity. In addition, these carbon dots present a reasonable reversible thermal response upon temperature, during heating and cooling ramps. The results of the temperature dependence of the average lifetime in the range of 20–60°C are in agreement with the literature where the average lifetimes of the carbon dots also decrease with the temperature between 77 and 300K [14].

Both parameters, average lifetimes and FWHM of the emission band, can be used to characterize the temperature from the practical point of view as nanosensors, however the FWHW shows a better sensitivity and the experimental requirement to obtain it is simpler. There are other quantum dots with better sensitivity to the temperature, as CdTe quantum dots [32]. In these quantum dots a change from 7nm to 1nm in the size produced an increase about a factor 10 in the sensitivity. Therefore, a detail analysis of the temperature sensitivity of the CDs with their size could improve this parameter, which together with its low toxicity and high biocompatibility make these materials very interesting for biological applications.

The emission and average lifetime of Carbon dots prepared from oxidation of candle shoot followed by reduction, has been obtained as function of the temperature in the 20–60°C. It has been found that the average lifetime appreciably changes with the wavelength emission. Besides, the temperature dependence of the average lifetime obtained at 515nm showed noticeable changes, decreasing with the increase of the temperature range between 20°C and 60°C. On the other hand, the FWHM of the emission spectrum increase notably in the same range of temperature (approximately 0.16nm/°C). In view of these significant changes of the average lifetimes and of the FWHM with the temperature and of the high biocompatibility of the Carbon dots, it is evident that these systems are promising temperature nanosensors for in vivo physiological applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was partially supported by Indo-Spanish Joint Programme of Cooperation in Science and Technology (DST-INT-Spain-P-38-11/PRI-PIBIN-2011-1153), MICINN (MAT2016-46649-C4-4-P), the Consolider-Ingenio 2010 Program MALTA (CSD2007-0045), and by the EU-FEDER. M.A.Hernández-Rodríguez thanks to MICINN by FPI grant.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jlumin.2017.12.062.
